title: The Macro Minute: Has the secular bear market in stocks begun already?

Happy holidays out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Monday,
December 29th, 2025. Hope everyone's
having a wonderful holiday season with
their family. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
leadoff morning note. So, let's hop
right in. Today's Q macro question is,
has the secular bare market and stocks
begun already? The short answer is not
likely, but it's coming. Don't try to
time it unless you're willing to risk a
lifestyle altering type two error in
your portfolio because you sold too
early. eventually succumbed to FOMO and
bought the pending market cycle top.
Terms of analytical nuance, sellside
strategists uniformly anticipate the US
stock market extending gains into 2026
for a fourth consecutive year, a run
that will represent the longest streak
of annual advances since 2007.
The mean year-end 2026 S&P 500 target
currently signals an additional 9%
upside from last price. The most
aggressive projections would deliver the
first instance of four straight years of
double-digit S&P 500 returns since 1999.
None of the 21 strategist pulled by
Bloomberg News projected a negative
annual return. The last time Wall Street
strategists were this aggressively and
uniformly bullish was in late 2024 ahead
of a turbulent 2025 when earlier
drawdowns driven by concerns over Deep
Seek's competitive threat to USI leaders
and President Donald Trump's erratic
trade war put optimistic projections at
risk. After the S&P 500 entered the then
non- consensus 42 macro paradigm B bare
market by falling 20% between
midFebruary and early April, strategists
cut their targets at the fastest rate
since the COVID crash before reversing
course as equities mounted one of the
quickest recoveries on record dating
back to the 1950s. said it differently,
Wall Street strategist sold the lows in
April only to be forced into the upside
capitulation, which is exactly what we
called for when we predicted uh which is
exactly what we predicted when we
kickstarted the 42 macro paradigm C bull
market in late April and early May.
Reminder, it's very important.
slowing down for dramatic effect.
The S&P 500 declined 57%
and took 5 and a half years to recover
its October 2007 high due to the global
financial crisis. The NASDAQ 100
declined 83% and took 15 1.5 years to
recover its March 2000 high due to the
dotcom bust. There will likely be a
secular bare market after the AI capex
bubble peaks, just as there was after
the railroad, consumer durable goods,
and internet capex bubbles. The panic of
1873 led to the long depression. The
1929 stock market crash led to the Great
Depression, and the.com bust led to the
early 2000's jobless recovery and
housing bubble, which gave way to the
global financial crisis. If your goal as
an investor is to retire on time and
comfortably or help your clients achieve
this goal, now would be a great time to
secure your partnership with a
highquality risk manager. Don't wait
until you're suffering from FOMO or FOMO
rather, which is fear of more losses and
debating whether to quote unquote ride
it out or puke the lows. That lifestyle
altering choice is coming for many
unsuspecting investors around the world.
I guarantee. As always, members of our
global investor community can trust that
our institutional-grade risk management
overlays, Kiss and Dr. Mo, will help our
portfolios navigate these emergent risks
better than investors attempting to
manage risk without systematic rules or
relying on fundamental research alone.
Transition to our 42 micro dashboard. As
always, I wrap up with a question from
our community. This one's titled goal.
Says, "Hi, Darius. If you're expecting
expecting a disinflationary impulse from
higher productivity growth which would
be sustained by AI impacts and lead to
even more Fed rate cuts fueling
disinflationary forces, are you
beginning to look at that goal position
in KISS and considering move back to
fixed income? Uh seeing as the market is
a discounting mechanism and will tend to
price shifts in far before they actually
occur. Uh no, I do not think the
geopolitically driven supply demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market is
concluded. It's not over. We have at
least another 5 to 10 years of dealing
with this condition because the the
underlying dynamic that's causing the
condition which is inequality which is
fueling populism which is fueling
chronic undertaxation by the political
right and chronic overspending by the
political left has not gone anywhere.
So don't be so myopic about your market
cycle calls about what's about to happen
over the next few quarters and allow it
to distract you from the longer run uh
dynamic that we ultimately need to
resolve. This is a problem that we the
US has a debt disease whether you like
it or not we are capitalizing oursel at
about 40% of global savings up from 20%
of global savings prior to co that delta
is a big problem in the context of
foreign creditors owning about a third
of the US marketable treasury market and
private non-bank investors now owning
about 60% of the marketable treasury
market which is almost a double from
where they were in late 2021.
These are big-time problems that don't
have easy solutions. And that's why we
say cut, grow, print is the only thing
that we're going to do to fix this. And
it may not even fix anything. Again,
we're in a fourth turning. The US might
not even survive this fourth turning as
a country. As ridiculous as that sounds,
we almost didn't survive the American
Revolution. We almost didn't survive the
Civil War intact. We we very clearly
barely survived the Great Depression and
World War II. So to assume that we're
going to just have a fantastically
positive outcome and now is the time to
start ignoring the geopolitically driven
supply demand imbalance in the Treasury
bond market because gold's gone up a lot
this year. I don't know. I just think
that's a very aggressive call that I'm
not willing to make. That's the
architect of a lot of this, you know,
thought leadership across global Wall
Street. I think it's too early. Um, you
know, I think it's too early. You know,
the the move in the central bank
allocations to gold is, you know,
relative to the US dollar allocation to
gold. I think gold's in the low 20%
range, dollars in the in the mid-40s.
Those numbers are wrong in our opinion.
The, you know, somewhere between 1 to
five or 5 to 10% asset allocation uh
that, you know, these major Wall Street
institutions have across their wealth
management platforms to gold is wrong.
It's too low. And so, in our view, we
think the bull market and bullion is
likely to persist over at least the next
several years. Doesn't have to go up in
a straight line, by the way. Markets
don't go anywhere in a straight line.
Your expectation can't be that you're
going to make money on every trade every
single day, every single week, every
single month. That is a nonsensical
expectation.
Ostensibly, you're a smart rich person
if you're an investor. So don't have
ridiculous expectations like that. But
you know what the beauty of KISS is in
terms of countering such a a ridiculous
expectation that gold has to go up every
day. I'm not saying this this person is
suggesting that. I'm just I'm just kind
of hypothetically suggesting this. The
beauty of KISS is that when the top down
risk management overlay, which actually
doesn't impact gold, when the bottom up
risk management overlay starts to take
our dynamic position sizing to gold
down, we rotate into cash and we use uh
short-term treasuries or you can use
money market fund exposure, you use T
bills, whatever you want. We use
short-term floating rate treasuries uh
as our cash uh instrument in KISS. So
that means when gold is not performing,
we're actually clipping coupons and
collecting yield with that cash. There's
no opportunity cost there. And so that's
the beauty of what we built here. Um
eventually there'll be a time where you
know the pace of monetary debasement and
financial repression uh that we are
highly likely to see um throughout
paradigm D uh in this uh in the context
of this geopolitically driven supply
demand imbalance in the Treasury bond
market. Maybe that stuff will slow down
after we kind of get to a crescendo. Um,
which may include a total war. By the
way, all the other for turnings have
since since the 1400s.
[laughter]
I don't know. I'm not smart enough to
guess that outcome, but uh, you know,
predict that outcome. But I I tell you
right now, we are smart enough to design
what I think continue to think and what
many thousands of investors around the
world think is the best retirement
solution on the planet. It's the only
retirement solution simple that's simple
and offers it retail investors
institutional grade risk management and
so I don't live in fear that gold is
peing and is about to peak because if it
is peing and is about to peak for you
know extended period of time then we'll
be clipping coupons and floating rate
treasuries instead.
It's called fantastic systems design. So
uh we'll wrap it up there. Uh we'll just
